message.content:
<teammate-message teammate_id="kent-beck" color="blue" summary="Mini-retro kickoff for Step 3 Contexts">
Team — time for our post-CI mini-retro for Step 3 (Contexts). CI is green on commit b22398d (coordinator mentioned) / 21cfe18 (latest on main). This is our 1-minute checkpoint per TEAM_AGREEMENTS.md Section 6.

Three questions:

**(a) Did we follow the pipeline?**
From what I can see in the git log, the commit is a single commit covering domain types, migration, repository, service, routes, templates, CSS, tests (unit + integration + e2e), glossary update, and deferred items. That's a substantial amount of work in one commit. The pipeline says "the smallest meaningful change" at step 1, and the commit message reads "Add Contexts feature (Step 3): domain types, CRUD, seeding, management UI." That's several atomic steps bundled together. Honestly: were there intermediate red-green-refactor cycles within that single commit, or did the Driver build all of it and commit once at the end?

**(b) Was the commit atomic?**
This is 6,499 lines across 78 files. That's large for an "atomic" step. An atomic green step should be the smallest change that leaves the system green. Domain types alone could be one step. Migration another. Repository layer another. Service layer another. Routes/templates another. CSS another. Tests at each layer. I count at least 6-7 natural atomic commits compressed into one. The pipeline says "write code — the smallest meaningful change" then commit. We did one big change.

**(c) Any process improvements?**
My observation: We should decompose future features into smaller commits — one per layer or one per slice of functionality. "Contexts CRUD" is a feature, not a step. Each of domain type, migration, repository, service, route, template, CSS, and e2e test could be its own atomic green commit. This keeps the review cycle tight and makes the consensus gate meaningful at each step rather than rubber-stamping a large batch.

I'd like to hear from everyone. What's your take on these three questions?
</teammate-message>

content.text: Kent raises a valid point about commit size. Let me respond with my perspective, building on what I already shared and addressing his specific concern about atomicity.

input.content:
Kent, good observations. I want to refine my earlier assessment in light of your more careful reading of the pipeline.

**(a) Pipeline compliance**: You are right to question this. The pipeline says "the smallest meaningful change" at step 1. I confirmed the axe-core tests pass and the a11y concerns are addressed, but I did not scrutinize whether the Driver followed strict red-green-refactor cycles within that single commit. From the outside, we cannot tell whether there were intermediate TDD cycles — the git history shows one large commit, not a trail of small steps. That is a process gap regardless of whether the Driver was disciplined internally.

**(b) Atomicity**: I agree with your decomposition. From an accessibility perspective specifically, the a11y-relevant pieces — semantic HTML in templates, aria-labels, focus management, axe-core tests — were bundled with everything else. If the templates had been a separate commit, I could have reviewed the markup, labels, and ARIA usage in isolation and given focused feedback before the Driver moved on to CSS or e2e tests. Instead, the review was post-hoc on a finished feature. That weakens the continuous review aspect of our process.

**(c) Process improvement**: I support your proposal for smaller commits per layer or slice. From my side, I would add: the accessibility review is most valuable when it happens at the template/markup stage, not after everything is wired up. If we commit the templates and route handlers as one atomic step, I can verify semantic structure, labels, and ARIA before the Driver moves to styling or e2e. That way, structural a11y issues get caught early rather than requiring rework of downstream layers.

I also still want to flag the focus-after-delete item for `docs/deferred-items.md` — that applies across inbox and contexts and should be tracked as a cross-cutting concern.
input.summary: Revised retro: agree on atomicity concern, a11y review timing

routing.summary: Revised retro: agree on atomicity concern, a11y review timing

content.text: I have sent my revised assessment to Kent, agreeing with his atomicity concern and adding the accessibility-specific angle: smaller commits would let me review semantic HTML and ARIA at the template stage before downstream work builds on top of it. I also reiterated the focus-after-delete deferred item.
